headlines.geopolitics: American guns flooding Mexican cartels through legal gun shops, shows, and apps
headlines.economy: Driverless big rigs hitting Texas highways as early as next year
headlines.domestic: ICE busts 'deeply disturbing' monkey torture ring in Florida

metadata.daily_do: Check if your state has universal background check laws and contact your representatives about closing private sale loopholes

clips.caption: Duke drops some uncomfortable truths about gun trafficking

script.text: Look, I'm as Second Amendment as they come, but when our guns are literally funding the people shooting at our border agents, we've got a problem.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros. It's March 17th, and here's what you need to know. First up, geopolitics.

A New York Times investigation reveals American firearms are surging across the border to Mexican cartels through legal gun shops, gun shows, websites, and apps. These weapons are fueling Mexico's most violent crimes, highlighting massive gaps in our cross-border enforcement.
Wait, through apps? Like, is there a Tinder for gun running now?
This is exactly what we predicted would happen with the private sale loopholes.
Hold on, let's get the facts first before we start policy prescriptions.

Economy. Autonomous big rig trucks are entering active testing on Texas highways. Multiple companies are banking on market entry as early as next year, representing a major shift toward driverless freight.
Jenny's gonna love this. Her brother drives for FedEx.
Three and a half million truck drivers in this country. What's the transition plan?
The transition plan is thoughts and prayers, Bree. Welcome to capitalism.

Domestic news. ICE investigators busted what they're calling a 'deeply disturbing' animal cruelty operation in Florida. Francisco Javier Ravelo pleaded guilty to distributing over forty videos depicting torture of baby and adult monkeys.
Oh my god.
Good heavens.
Seven years isn't enough.
I'm not making jokes about this one. That's genuinely sick.
Ravelo was prosecuted under the PACT Act, which President Trump signed during his first term. He faces up to seven years in federal prison.
Let's dig into the gun trafficking story because this cuts across everything we argue about. Bree, you've been following weapons flow for years. What's your take?

This is the predictable result of having the weakest gun laws in the developed world combined with the strongest gun market. We've created a system where it's easier to buy a semi-automatic rifle than to adopt a dog.
Come on. It's not easier to buy a rifle than adopt a dog. You need a background check for—
Only from licensed dealers! Private sales, gun shows, online platforms — no background check required in most states.
And that's a problem we should fix. I'm not defending straw purchases or trafficking. But let's be clear about what's actually happening here.
What do you mean?

Most of these guns aren't being bought by cartel members walking into Bass Pro Shop. They're being purchased by American citizens who then illegally transfer them across the border. That's already a federal crime.
Right, but it's a federal crime that's almost impossible to prosecute because we don't track private sales!
I'll give you that. Universal background checks make sense for this exact reason.
Wait, did Duke just agree with me on gun policy?
Somebody check the temperature in hell.
Look, I'm as Second Amendment as they come, but when our guns are literally funding the people shooting at our border agents, we've got a problem.
The Times investigation found that cartels are specifically requesting American weapons because they're higher quality than what they can get elsewhere.
Of course they are. We make the best guns in the world. That's not exactly a secret.
So what's your solution? Because right now we're basically the world's largest arms dealer to criminal organizations.
Close the private sale loophole. Mandatory background checks for all transfers. But also — and this is important — actually enforce the laws we already have.
The ATF has been systematically underfunded for decades. They can't enforce laws they don't have the resources to investigate.
That's on Congress. Both parties have treated the ATF like a political football instead of a law enforcement agency.
Wait, are we actually having a reasonable gun control conversation? Did someone slip something in the coffee?
The investigation also found that some licensed dealers are clearly turning a blind eye to obvious straw purchases.
Then prosecute them. Pull their licenses. If you're knowingly selling to traffickers, you're not a legitimate gun dealer, you're a criminal.
I agree completely. But here's what frustrates me — we've known about this problem for twenty years. The data has been there. We just haven't had the political will to act.

Because every time someone proposes a reasonable solution, both sides immediately go to their extremes. Gun control advocates want to ban everything, gun rights advocates won't accept any new restrictions.
That's not— okay, that's partially fair. But the NRA has blocked even basic research into gun violence for decades.
The Dickey Amendment was stupid policy. You can't solve problems you're not allowed to study.
So what would actually work here? Practically speaking.
Universal background checks, better ATF funding, and real penalties for dealers who look the other way. Also, Mexico needs to crack down on their end.
All of that, plus we need to actually secure the border. It's a lot harder to smuggle weapons if we know who's crossing and where.
I'm sorry, did Bree and Duke just solve gun violence in America? Should we call Biden?
We didn't solve gun violence. We identified some obvious steps that should have been taken years ago.
And we'll probably forget this conversation happened the next time there's a mass shooting and everyone goes back to their corners.
Well, let's at least document that it happened. Professor, this has to have historical parallels, right?
Oh my, yes. Though perhaps not the parallel people expect.

Everyone will immediately think of Iran-Contra, and there are similarities — American weapons flowing to unauthorized recipients, government agencies looking the other way. But the more instructive parallel is the Neutrality Acts of the 1930s.
I'm not familiar with those.

Congress passed a series of laws in the thirties designed to keep America out of foreign conflicts by restricting arms sales. The problem was, American weapons manufacturers were making enormous profits selling to both sides of various wars.
The Nye Committee — Senator Gerald Nye of North Dakota — investigated and found that arms dealers were actively encouraging conflicts to boost sales. They called them 'merchants of death.'
Okay, but that's different. Those were legal sales to foreign governments.
Ah, but here's where it gets interesting. The committee found that many supposedly 'legal' sales were actually going to unauthorized buyers through elaborate shell companies and middlemen.
American companies would sell to, say, a legitimate dealer in Guatemala, knowing full well those weapons would immediately be resold to rebels in Nicaragua. Plausible deniability, you see.
So basically the same scam, just with more paperwork.
Precisely. The Neutrality Acts tried to close these loopholes by requiring government approval for all arms exports. It worked, mostly.
What happened to the legislation?

World War Two. Once we needed to arm Britain and the Soviet Union against Hitler, neutrality became a luxury we couldn't afford. But the regulatory framework remained — that's how we got the Arms Export Control Act.
The lesson, I think, is that when American weapons are destabilizing our neighbors, it eventually becomes our problem whether we acknowledge it or not.
And the cartels are definitely destabilizing Mexico.
Which affects border security, immigration, trade — everything. Professor's right, it becomes our problem.
The merchants of death inquiry led to real reform because Congress finally understood that unrestricted arms sales were a national security threat. Perhaps it's time for a similar reckoning.
A modern Nye Committee for gun trafficking. I like that.
As long as they don't call it the 'merchants of death' committee. The branding is a little intense.
Alright, time for Rapid Fire, where the news gets weird and I get to make inappropriate jokes about it.
First up: A drunk US general left classified maps on a train after getting a concussion in Kyiv. The maps apparently detailed troop movements in Eastern Europe.
Please tell me this is satire.
I wish. The general's excuse was that he got concussed during a missile attack and then 'self-medicated' with alcohol. The maps were found by a janitor.
That's a court martial.
That's also probably how Russia gets half their intelligence.
Next: Scientists have figured out how to turn plastic waste into Parkinson's medicine using modified bacteria. Finally, a use for all those Amazon packages.
Wait, that's actually incredible. Circular economy meets medical innovation.
Yeah, apparently the bacteria eat the plastic and excrete the precursor compounds for L-DOPA. It's like if your recycling bin became a pharmacy.
Remarkable. Though I do worry about the quality control implications.
Moving on to Florida, because of course. The state legislature failed to ban cousin marriage this year. Democracy in action, folks.
I'm sorry, what?
A bill to ban first-cousin marriage died in committee. Apparently it wasn't a priority this session. They were too busy with important legislation like, uh...
Banning books and restricting drag shows?
Exactly! Priorities, people. Can't have men in dresses reading to children, but cousins getting married? That's just family values.
Moving on.
And finally, a job rejection story that perfectly captures late-stage capitalism. Someone in California got rejected for a position because, quote, 'Your car is too old for this position.'
That can't be legal.
It was for a sales position where the candidate would be visiting clients. The company said a 2018 Honda Civic didn't 'project the right image.'
A 2018 Civic is eight years old. That's not even old.
Right? I'm still driving a 2019 Corolla and it runs fine. Apparently I'm unemployable now.
This is why we need stronger worker protection laws. Discrimination based on personal property should be illegal.
Or maybe companies should pay enough that their employees can afford the cars they apparently require.
Revolutionary concept, Duke. Pay people enough to meet your arbitrary status requirements.
Alright, time for the Daily Do. Given today's gun trafficking story, here's something concrete you can actually do.
Check if your state has universal background check laws. Eighteen states plus DC require background checks for all handgun sales, but only eleven require them for all firearms including rifles.
If your state doesn't have universal background checks, contact your representatives. This isn't about taking anyone's guns — it's about making sure we know who's buying them.
And if you're in a state that does have them, check if they're actually being enforced. Some states have the laws but don't fund enforcement.
This is one of those rare issues where most Americans actually agree. Ninety percent support universal background checks in polling.
The disconnect between public opinion and policy is massive on this one. Your representatives need to hear from you.
As Cicero wrote, 'The authority of those who teach is often an obstacle to those who want to learn.' Sometimes the people lead and the politicians follow.
And on that inspirational note from the Professor, I'm Jax Mooney reminding you that democracy is like a 2018 Honda Civic — it's not flashy, but it gets the job done if you maintain it properly.
We'll be back tomorrow. I'm Leo Barros.
Bree Aldana.
Duke Patel.
Dr. Haywood Pringle.
And I'm Jax Mooney. This has been The Friction.
